elements.sent-2411-001: In many applications, especially due to lack of supervision or privacy concerns, the training data is grouped into bags of instances (feature-vectors) and for each bag we have only an aggregate label derived from the instance-labels in the bag.
elements.sent-2411-002: In learning from label proportions (LLP) the aggregate label is the average of the instance-labels in a bag, and a significant body of work has focused on training models in the LLP setting to predict instance-labels.
elements.sent-2411-003: In practice however, the training data may have fully supervised albeit covariate-shifted source data, along with the usual target data with bag-labels, and we wish to train a good instance-level predictor on the target domain.
elements.sent-2411-004: We call this the covariate-shifted hybrid LLP problem.
elements.sent-2411-005: Fully supervised covariate shifted data often has useful training signals and the goal is to leverage them for better predictive performance in the hybrid LLP setting.
elements.sent-2411-006: To achieve this, we develop methods for hybrid LLP which naturally incorporate the target bag-labels along with the source instance-labels, in the domain adaptation framework.
elements.sent-2411-007: Apart from proving theoretical guarantees bounding the target generalization error, we also conduct experiments on several publicly available datasets showing that our methods outperform LLP and domain adaptation baselines as well techniques from previous related work.
elements.sent-2411-008: Learning from label proportions (LLP) is a direct generalization of supervised learning where the training instances (i.e., feature-vectors) are partitioned into bags and for each bag only the average label of its instances is available as the bag-label.
elements.sent-2411-009: Full supervision is equivalent to the special case of unit-sized bags.
elements.sent-2411-010: In LLP, using bags of instances and their bag-labels, the goal is to train a good predictor of the instance-labels.
elements.sent-2411-011: Over the last two decades, LLP has been used in scenarios with lack of fully supervised data due to legal requirements~\cite{R10 , privacy constraints~\cite{WIBB or coarse supervision~\cite{CHR .}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Applications of LLP include image classification~\cite{Bortsova18, Orting16 , spam detection~\cite{QSCL09 , IVF prediction~\cite{hernandez2018 , and high energy physics~\cite{DNRS .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: More recently, restrictions on cross-site tracking of users has led to coarsening of previously available fine-grained signals which have been used to train large-scale models predicting user behavior for e.g. clicks or product preferences.
elements.sent-2411-014: Popular mechanisms (see Apple SKAN~\cite{skan and Chrome Privacy sandbox~\cite{sandbox ) aggregate relevant labels for bags of users resulting in LLP training data.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Due to revenue criticality of user modeling in advertising, the study of LLP specifically for such applications has gained importance.
elements.sent-2411-016: A popular baseline method to train models using training bags and their bag-labels is to minimize a bag-level loss which for any bag is some suitable loss function between the the average prediction and the bag-label (see \cite{ArdehalyC17 ).}
elements.sent-2411-017: Other methods using different bag-level losses have also been proposed (e.g. \cite{OT,fast-llp ) for training models in the LLP setting.}
elements.sent-2411-018: One aspect of data in real-world applications is its heterogeneity, which introduces new aspects to the vanilla LLP modeling formulation.
elements.sent-2411-019: In particular, apart from bag-level data from the target distribution, the learner may have access to instance labels from a covariate-shifted source distribution corresponding for e.g. to online users who have consented to share their labels or to data with less restrictive regulations.
elements.sent-2411-020: Here, we think of covariate-shift as a difference in $p(\mb{X )$ -- the distribution of feature-vectors -- while keeping $p(Y\mid\mb{X )$ the same.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: We call this covariate-shifted hybrid LLP in which the goal is to leverage the full supervision on the source as well as the bag-level supervision on the target to train better instance-label predictors on the target distribution.
elements.sent-2411-022: Previous work of \cite{Domain-Adaption-AC,Li-Culotta studied the case where the source training data was aggregated into bags whose bag-labels are available, while the training data from the target distribution is completely unsupervised.}
elements.sent-2411-023: The work of \cite{Domain-Adaption-AC gave a self-training based approach where the model trained on the source data is used to predict bag-labels on the unsupervised target train-set from which a subset of the most confidently labeled bags are used to (along with the source data) retrain the predictor.}
elements.sent-2411-024: The more recent work of \cite{Li-Culotta proposed solutions directly applying domain adversarial neural-network (DANN) methods in which apart from minimizing the bag-level loss on the source data, an unsupervised domain prediction loss is maximized to ensure that the predictor is domain-independent.}
elements.sent-2411-025: The work of \cite{Domain-Adaption-AC,Li-Culotta as well as standard domain adaptation methods (for e.g. \cite{long2015learning ) can be applied to our setting by simply ignoring the bag-labels of the target train-set, and treating the labeled instances in the source data as bags of size $1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Note however that these approaches discard the informative signal from the target bag-labels and are thus likely to degrade the predictive performance.
elements.sent-2411-027: The main contribution of this paper is a suite of techniques which use the bag-labels from the target training set, not only to minimize the bag-loss i.e., the predictive loss on bags, but also to do better domain adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-028: We focus on regression as the underlying task and propose loss functions which, at a high level, have three components: (i) the instance-level loss on the source data, (ii) a bag-level loss on the target training bags, and (iii) a domain adaptation loss which leverages the instance-labels from source and bag-labels from target.
elements.sent-2411-029: We substantially extend to our setting previous techniques for domain adaptation as well as LLP, and prove generalization error bounds which justify our loss formulations.
elements.sent-2411-030: Complementing these analytical insights, we provide extensive experimental evaluations of our methods showing performance gains, on real as well as synthetic datasets.

elements.sent-2411-112: The first method uses ${\sf BagCSI}$ loss as the objective.
elements.sent-2411-113: We have shown above that ${\sf BagCSI}$ loss is an upper bound over $\eps(\mc{D _T, h)$ loss w.r.t target distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-114: We now provide intuitive explanation for why ${\sf BagCSI}$ loss should work.
elements.sent-2411-115: Let us assume that the goal is to predict label for an unseen instance $\bx$, given feature representations $\phi(\bx_i)$ in the embedding space and corresponding labels $y_i$ from training data.
elements.sent-2411-116: A natural prediction would be $\E_i \left[\rho(\phi(\bx), \phi(\bx_i))y_i\right]$, where $\rho$ is some similarity metric.
elements.sent-2411-117: If we choose the similarity metric to be the inner product, the prediction can be written as $\phi(\bx)^{\sf T}\E_i \left[\phi(\bx_i)y_i\right]$.
elements.sent-2411-118: The given feature representations and corresponding labels can come either from the source domain or from the target domain.
elements.sent-2411-119: For learning domain invariant feature representation, the prediction should be similar irrespective of the domain considered.
elements.sent-2411-120: This can be achieved by enforcing the term, $\sum\limits_i y_i\phi(\bx_i)$ to be equal for source and target domain.
elements.sent-2411-121: However, this approach requires knowledge of instance-level labels $y_\bx$ from target domain, which are not available.
elements.sent-2411-122: We can however replace $y_\bx$ with pseudo-labels $\hat{y _\bx$, using which we introduce a new domain adaptation loss term in the objective, $\psi^2(\mc{S , \mc{B )$ where: \begin{equation}\psi(\mc{S}, \mc{B}) := \frac{1}{mk}\left\|\sum_{j=1}^m \sum_{\bx \in B_j}\hat{y}_\bx\phi(\bx) - \sum_{i=1}^{mk}y_i\phi(\bz_i) \right\|_2\end{equation} One way is to assign the bag-label as the pseudo-label for all instances withing the bag, in which case $\psi(\mc{S , \mc{B )$ essentially reduces to $\xi(\mc{S , \mc{B )$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-123: We call this method Bag Label Weighted Feature Alignment (\textbfne{BL-WFA ) which involves training using the ${\sf BagCSI}$ loss.}
elements.sent-2411-124: Another approach is to use the following process for pseudo-labeling instances in a bag $B$ using hypothesis model $h$:.
elements.sent-2411-126: The pseudo-labels are given by adding to each prediction the same $b \in \R$ such that average pseudo-label in the bag equals the bag-label.
elements.sent-2411-127: Note that this is equivalent to the nearest vector of pseudo-labels (in Euclidean distance) to the vector predictions, that satisfies the bag-label constraint.
elements.sent-2411-128: We call this method Pseudo-label Weighted Feature Alignment (\textbfne{PL-WFA ) in which $\psi(\mc{S , \mc{B )$ is used to train the model using the above computed pseudo-labels.}}}

elements.sent-2411-129: We evaluate our approaches via experiments on both synthetic as well as real-world datasets and compare against the baselines for different bag sizes.
elements.sent-2411-130: In \cite{Li-Culotta , authors propose methods for domain adaptation in LLP setting for classification tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-131: We adapt these methods for regression tasks and consider those as baselines.
elements.sent-2411-132: In this paper, these baselines are referred to as Average Feature (AF), Label Regularization (LR), Average Feature DANN (AF-DANN) and Label Regularization DANN (LR-DANN).
elements.sent-2411-133: See section 3.1.2, 3.1.3, 3.2.1, 3.2.2 in \cite{Li-Culotta for respective methods.}
elements.sent-2411-134: In literature on domain adaptation (for non-LLP settings) \cite{long2015learning, long2017deep , it has been shown that approaches using MMD (maximum mean discrepancy) based objectives work well.}
elements.sent-2411-135: Hence, we also define a baseline that uses similar objective adapted for our setting, called Domain Mean Feature Alignment (DMFA).
elements.sent-2411-136: We also consider bag level target loss (\textbfne{Bagged-Target ) as a baseline.}
elements.sent-2411-137: Appendix \ref{app:baselines contains additional details about baseline methods.}
elements.sent-2411-138: We evaluate and compare our methods against these baselines.
elements.sent-2411-139: Our model training uses the above losses in a mini-batch loop.
elements.sent-2411-140: For DMFA and PL-WFA we select equal number of instances from both source and target domain in a mini-batch.
elements.sent-2411-141: For BL-WFA, we select as many instances from source domain as the number of bags selected from target domain in a mini-batch.
elements.sent-2411-142: Such a choice avoids explicit normalization in the objective function and incorporates them into the hyper-parameters.
elements.sent-2411-143: We evaluate all the baselines and proposed methods for different bag sizes and datasets.
elements.sent-2411-144: All datasets are split into two components, source and target domain.
elements.sent-2411-145: For our study, it is important that there is a reasonable covariate shift between these two components.
elements.sent-2411-146: The target domain dataset is split into train (80\%) and test (20\%) sets.
elements.sent-2411-147: The train set is partitioned randomly into bags of equal size.
elements.sent-2411-148: The synthetic dataset has 64 dimensional continuous feature vectors and scalar-valued continuous label.
elements.sent-2411-149: For covariate shifted source and target domain data, the feature vectors are sampled from a multi-dimensional Gaussian distribution with different means and covariance matrices.
elements.sent-2411-150: The labels for both source and target data are computed using the same randomly initialized neural network.
elements.sent-2411-151: We also evaluate methods on 2 real world datasets: Wine Ratings dataset \cite{wine_ratings, wine_reviews and IPUMS USA dataset \cite{ipums_usa_2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-152: For wine dataset, the source domain comprises of wines from France and the target domain comprises of wines from all countries but France.
elements.sent-2411-153: For IPUMS dataset, we select a subset of feature columns comprising of the following features: REGION, STATEICP, AGE, IND, GQ, SEX and WKSWORK2 and use INCWAGE as the labels.
elements.sent-2411-154: We consider the data from 1970 as the source domain and data from 2022 as the target domain.
elements.sent-2411-156: We use a simple neural network comprising of an input layer followed by two sequential ReLU activated layers (128 nodes) and a final linear layer (1 node).
elements.sent-2411-157: For US Census, dataset, we additionally include embedding layers after the input layer for all the cardinal and categorical features that were not converted to one-hot representations.
elements.sent-2411-158: For AF-DANN and LR-DANN, we also have a sigmoid activated domain prediction layer in parallel to the final dense layer.
elements.sent-2411-159: During training, we perform a grid search to find the most optimal set of hyperparameters for each configuration (specific dataset, methodology and bag size).
elements.sent-2411-160: We try out two different optimizers for all configurations - Adam and SGD and report scores corresponding to the best performer.
elements.sent-2411-161: For each configuration, we run the same experiment multiple times and report the MSE scores on target domain's test data as the evaluation metric.
elements.sent-2411-162: Note that the instances in target domain are randomly bagged for each run.
elements.sent-2411-163: The final evaluation metric is reported by the mean and standard deviation over these runs.
elements.sent-2411-164: Specifically, we run 20 trials for each configuration with Wine and Synthetic dataset and 10 trials for each configuration with US Census dataset.
elements.sent-2411-165: A different random seed is chosen for each trial.
elements.sent-2411-166: We will release the code for our experiments along with the final version of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-167: Our experiments were run on a system with standard 8-core CPU, 256GB of memory with one P100 GPU.
elements.sent-2411-168: The evaluation metrics are reported for all 3 datasets in tables \ref{tab:wine , \ref{tab:usc and \ref{tab:synthetic .}}}
elements.sent-2411-169: For bag size 256, BL-WFA achieves 2.9\%, 2.5\% and 27.9\% improvement over the best baseline method for Wine, US Census and synthetic datasets respectively.
elements.sent-2411-170: MSE is used as evaluation metric, hence scores cannot be compared across datasets due to different scales.
elements.sent-2411-172: We make the following inferences from the results:.
elements.sent-2411-173: PL-WFA and BL-WFA consistently outperform all other baselines for large enough bag sizes.
elements.sent-2411-174: This is expected because with increase in bag size, the information from just the bagged target domain is not rich enough and benefits greatly from inclusion of covariate shifted source domain data.
elements.sent-2411-175: By leveraging not just the features from target domain but also the bagged-labels, PL-WFA and BL-WFA outperform other baseline methods which rely only on features from target domain for domain adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-176: With increase in bag size, the performance drops.
elements.sent-2411-177: This is expected as information is lost with increase in bag size.
elements.sent-2411-178: On synthetic dataset (where we definitely have a reasonable amount of covariate shift), even with bag size as large as 256, we see that the performance of our proposed methods - PL-WFA and BL-WFA is better than the case where we use instance level labeled target data for training ( target instance loss).
elements.sent-2411-179: This improvement is achieved despite the fact that performance when just using the source data for training (source instance loss) is poor.
elements.sent-2411-180: On smaller bag sizes, other methods (for example, LR and DMFA on synthetic dataset and Bagged-Target on Wine dataset) seem to outperform our proposed methods.
elements.sent-2411-181: Such behavior is expected when the information from target data is itself sufficient to learn a good enough function approximator.
elements.sent-2411-182: It is worth noting that the objective function in our proposed method reduces to that of LR for $\lambda = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-183: So, in theory PL-WFA and BL-WFA are always better than LR.
elements.sent-2411-184: By decreasing the $\lambda$ value, our methods can do at least as good as LR.
elements.sent-2411-185: Although the best baseline method is different for different datasets under consideration (AF-DANN on Wine, LR on US Census and DMFA on synthetic), BL-WFA consistently beats the best baseline for a large enough bag size.
elements.sent-2411-186: We formally define the problem of learning from label aggregates where source data has instance wise labels while target data has aggregate labels of instances grouped into bags.
elements.sent-2411-187: We also give bag-to-instance generalization error bound for regression tasks in LLP and use it to arrive at BagCSI loss.
elements.sent-2411-188: We propose two new methods, BL-WFA (based on BagCSI) and PL-WFA (based on a variant of BagCSI) that naturally incorporate the knowledge of aggregate labels from target domain in the domain adaptation framework leading to improvement over baseline methods.
elements.sent-2411-189: We also adapt several methods from literature in domain adaptation and LLP to this setting.
elements.sent-2411-190: Through experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets we show that our methods consistenty outperform baseline techniques.. \bibliographystyle{apalike \onecolumn.}

elements.sent-2411-406: We test the performance of all the baselines and proposed methods when using a non-uniform bag size.
elements.sent-2411-407: The dataset is partitioned into bags of different sizes.
elements.sent-2411-408: More specifically, we use 2 different techniques to have mixed size bags:.
elements.sent-2411-410: For a particular bag size, there are an equal number of samples that belong to a bag of that size.
elements.sent-2411-411: Hence, if there are $n_1$ bags of size $k_1$, and $n_2$ bags of size $k_2$, then $n_1k_1=n_2k_2$.
elements.sent-2411-413: There are equal number of bags of each size.
elements.sent-2411-414: Hence, if there are $n_1$ bags of size $k_1$, and $n_2$ bags of size $k_2$, then $n_1=n_2$.
elements.sent-2411-415: Clearly, SBB will have more bags of smaller sizes compared to BBB.
elements.sent-2411-416: Every bag is of the size 8, 32, 128 or 256.
elements.sent-2411-417: Tables \ref{tab:bbb_mixed_bag_size and \ref{tab:sbb_mixed_bag_size contain the results for experiments with mixed bag sizes.}}
elements.sent-2411-418: It can be inferred from the results that the scores with mixed bag sizes are mostly an interpolation (not necessarily linear) of the results with uniform bag sizes.
elements.sent-2411-419: Scores with BBB strategy for mixing bags are worse compared to SBB since SBB has a higher proportion of small sized bags compared to BBB.
elements.sent-2411-420: We test the performance of all the baselines and proposed methods with correlated bags as opposed to random bags used for all other experiments in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-421: Since all the features in Wine dataset are binary, we did not perform experiments for it.
elements.sent-2411-422: We used REGION for IPUMS and product\_brand for Criteo SSCL as the correlated feature.
elements.sent-2411-423: Tables \ref{tab:correlated_ipums , \ref{tab:correlated_synthetic and \ref{tab:correlated_criteo contain results for experiments with correlated bags on IPUMS, Synthetic datasets and Criteo SSCL respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-424: It can be inferred that the standard deviation values are very low.
elements.sent-2411-425: This is expected because across different runs, similar bags would be created unlike experiments for un-correlated bags where the bags created for each run would comprise of a different set of instances.
elements.sent-2411-426: We also conduct experiments to analyze the impact on performance of different methods by varying the amount of covariate shift in the source and target domains of the synthetic datasets.
elements.sent-2411-427: The covariate shift can be controlled using the mean and standard deviation of the source and target distributions.
elements.sent-2411-428: The $\epsilon$ parameter is a measure of the the perturbation between the mean vectors of the source and target distributions, and $\delta$ is that for the perturbation between the covariance matrices.
elements.sent-2411-429: Specifically, a target distribution is given by a 64-dimensional Gaussian where each entry is iid, sampled from $N(50, 8)$ while $\Sigma$ is a diagonal matrix where each diagonal element is the magnitude of an iid value sampled from $N(10, 8)$.
elements.sent-2411-430: For each $(\epsilon, \delta)$, the source distribution is $N(\mu', \sigma')$ where $\mu'=\mu - \epsilon\Delta$ and $\Delta$ is a vector with iid values samples from $N(50, 8)$.
elements.sent-2411-431: The diagonal matrix $\Sigma'$ is obtained by adding the magnitude of value sampled from iid $N(0, 8\delta^2)$ to each diagonal entry of $\Sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-432: We perform experiments for different perturbations in the mean vector (using $\epsilon$) and covariance matrix (using $\delta$) of source and target distributions.
elements.sent-2411-433: As expected, with increasing perturbations, the scores become higher.
elements.sent-2411-434: Since MSE scores worsen more consistently with increase in mean perturbation as compared to perturbation in covariance matrix, we infer that the impact of increasing mean perturbation is more prominent compared to the perturbation in covariance matrix.
elements.sent-2411-435: Table \ref{tab:synth-perturbation contains scores for different combinations of perturbation values.}
